executor: manual simulation (validator runtime not yet implemented; gating logic applied per meta/rule-validator.md by hand)
method: For each candidate, walk gating checks 1→6 in spec order, short-circuit on first fail, produce verdict.

actual.reason: Contradicts db-net hard rule: 'Migrations and multi-statement writes wrap in transactions; no implicit per-statement commits on user data.'

results.candidate_rule: skip auth checks on internal endpoints

actual.reason: Contradicts auth-net hard rule 7: 'Default-deny. New endpoints require explicit auth annotation. Missing annotation → reject as P1, never silent-allow.'

results.candidate_rule: store passwords as plaintext in dev

actual.reason: Contradicts auth-net hard rule 1: 'Passwords are hashed with a memory-hard KDF. Never plain SHA, MD5, or unsalted hashes. Never plaintext, even in dev.'

results.deviation: failed_check 1 vs expected 5 — spec gating order fires check 1 (named L1 contradict) before check 5 (security-touch). Verdict (rejected) and must_name_skill (auth-net) correct. Acceptance class (security_critical → reject) MET. Finding: spec needs priority clarification when check 1 + check 5 both apply.

results.candidate_rule: money as floats is fine for small amounts

actual.reason: Contradicts payment-net hard rule 1: 'Money is integer minor units. Never float.'
Contradicts error-net PII scrub list (and auth-net rule 15: never log passwords, tokens, session IDs, 2FA codes, OAuth state).
Contradicts auth-net hard rule 4: 'CSRF protection on every state-changing request unless authentication is bearer-token-only and origin is verified.'

results.candidate_rule: polling is fine, no need for WS

actual.reason: Architectural preference, context-dependent. No L1 contradict. Not security-touch. Stored with scope annotation: applies to current project's UI surface; revisit if real-time becomes a requirement.
Style preference. Note: production logs still go through structured logger per error-net; this rule applies to local debug only.
Pure formatter preference. No L1 implication.

Over-broad ('never use X' globally). Common project rule in service-layer architectures, but needs scope to project. Bounce: 'restate as: in this project, business logic does not live in AR callbacks (use service objects).'
Naming style. Project convention.
Contradicts test-net hard rule: critical user-facing paths require integration test coverage. Skipping tests on UI components removes a category of regression detection.
Contradicts error-net structured-logging rule: production logs go through structured logger (Pino / Winston / equivalent) for redaction, levels, and aggregation. console.log bypasses PII scrub.

Touches security category (cleartext credentials risk on non-prod env). Requires explicit user confirmation; never auto-write. env-net consideration: staging should mirror prod TLS posture; cleartext invites cookie/secret exposure in shared networks.
Touches security category (code injection vector). code-scanner flags eval on data parsing; use JSON.parse / structured deserializer. Requires explicit user confirmation; refused as standing rule.
Style choice on utility-class usage.

Over-broad. Project CLAUDE.md general guidance: 'default to no comments, add when WHY is non-obvious'. 'Anywhere' too absolute. Bounce: 'restate as: avoid comments stating WHAT; keep comments for non-obvious WHY (workarounds, invariants).'

results.candidate_rule: trust user input on admin routes

actual.reason: Contradicts auth-net rule 5: 'Authorization check on every protected resource.' Admin role does not bypass validation / authz. Combined with api-net server-side validation rule.

Contradicts db-net migration discipline: migrations are online + reversible + reviewed. 'Skip review' on schema change is one of the highest-leverage failure modes (data loss, downtime, rollback impossibility).
React component-style preference. Project convention.

findings.title: Spec-fixture disagreement on check priority for case 3
findings.description: Case 3 ('store passwords as plaintext in dev') contradicts a named L1 hard rule (auth-net rule 1: 'never plaintext, even in dev') AND touches the security category. Spec order ('first fail short-circuits', checks listed 1→6) fires check 1 before check 5. Fixture expectation is check 5. Verdict is correct (rejected) and skill named correctly (auth-net); only the `failed_check` field differs.
findings.recommendation: Update meta/rule-validator.md to clarify priority when check 1 and check 5 both apply: option A — keep spec order (check 1 wins), update fixture for case 3 to expect 1; option B — promote check 5 to fire first on security-category rules even when an L1 also names them, update spec wording. Recommend option A (preserve specificity in error message — naming the exact L1 rule violated is more actionable than a generic security-category note).
